title: Smooth LED (PWM)
subtitle: Students modify delay and brightness values. See the difference.
overview: In this session, students discover how microcontrollers simulate analog brightness using Pulse Width Modulation (PWM). By modifying delay and brightness values in code, they directly observe how timing transforms a harsh blink into a smooth fade.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — component photos, activity shots, student experiments, whiteboard diagrams.

gallery.caption: Students tweaking values in code to change fading speed

keyTopics: Digital vs. Analog — Digital is ON/OFF (0 or 1), Analog is smooth (0% to 100%)
PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) — Cinema Trick! Blinking so fast that our human eyes see it as dimming
Duty Cycle — The percentage of time the electricity is turned ON vs turned OFF in a single cycle
The ~ Symbol — The tilde sign on Arduino that marks special pins capable of outputting PWM signals
analogWrite() — The magic instruction that sets PWM brightness from 0 (completely off) to 255 (fully bright)
Delay Modification — Changing code wait times to make the LED breathe faster or slower
Persistence of Vision (POV) — Why our brain is fooled into seeing continuous light instead of high-speed blinking

resources.title: Wokwi — Free Arduino PWM Fading Simulator

activity.title: The Breathing LED Challenge
parts: USB Cable & Computer with Arduino IDE/Wokwi
steps: Connect the resistor to the LED's long leg (Anode)
Connect the other end of the resistor specifically to a PWM Pin (like Pin 9, marked with ~)
Connect the LED's short leg (Cathode) to the Arduino GND pin
Upload the fade sketch and observe the LED 'breathing' smoothly
Challenge 1: Change the delay values in code to make the LED breathe super fast like a heartbeat!
Challenge 2: Limit the maximum brightness variable to 50 instead of 255 and see the difference

quiz.explanation: PWM stands for Pulse Width Modulation! It means modulating (adjusting) the width of electrical pulses to control power.

quiz.q: How does PWM make an LED look dimmer?
options: It physically lowers the battery voltage going to the LED
It turns the LED ON and OFF so fast that our eyes average it out
It increases the resistance inside the wire automatically
It changes the color of the LED light
quiz.explanation: PWM is a cinema trick! It blinks the LED hundreds of times per second. Because it's so fast, our eyes experience it as smooth dimming.

quiz.q: Which symbol marks PWM-capable pins on an Arduino Uno board?
quiz.explanation: Look for the small wave or tilde symbol (~) next to pins like 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, and 11 on your Arduino!

quiz.q: What range of values can we pass inside the analogWrite() function?
quiz.explanation: analogWrite uses 8-bit values, meaning it accepts numbers from 0 (0% duty cycle / Off) up to 255 (100% duty cycle / Fully On).

quiz.q: If an LED has a 10% Duty Cycle, how will it appear?
options: Very dim, because it is only ON for 10% of the time
It will blink slowly once every 10 seconds
quiz.explanation: A lower duty cycle means the electricity is ON for a short time and OFF for a long time, making the LED look very dim.

quiz.q: What happens if you decrease the delay value in a fading code script?
options: The LED stops fading and stays fully bright
The color changes from red to green
quiz.explanation: Smaller delay means the program waits less time between shifting brightness steps, making the LED breathe/fade much faster!

quiz.q: If you connect an LED to a normal digital pin (like Pin 7) and try analogWrite(), what happens?
options: It won't fade; it behaves like normal digitalWrite (either full ON or full OFF)
quiz.explanation: Normal digital pins cannot modulate pulse widths. They only understand absolute 0 or 1, so the LED will just turn ON or OFF without fading.

quiz.q: Which real-life scenario uses PWM technology?
options: Controlling the speed of a ceiling fan or drone motor
The transition of a smart ambient light fading to sleep mode
quiz.explanation: PWM is used everywhere! It controls screen brightness, dimming lamps, and regulating motor speeds smoothly.
